authoritative_sources.title: Selling on the Internet: Prompt Delivery Rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC is the primary U.S. consumer-protection authority for unfair or deceptive commerce practices. This guidance is authoritative for online-order promises, delays, cancellations, and prompt refunds when merchants miss commitments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: order, cancellation, and refund baseline review
used_for: delivery and pickup commitment caveats for reporting

authoritative_sources.title: Americans with Disabilities Act Title III Regulations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: DOJ's Title III ADA regulations are the authoritative federal public-accommodation baseline for lodging reservation systems, including accessible-room booking handling, which matters when PMS and reservation records affect the metric.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: reservation, booking, and guest-record baseline review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Handbook 130 - Current Edition
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST Office of Weights and Measures publishes the public baseline used by many U.S. jurisdictions for retail price verification and price-accuracy procedures, making it authoritative for menu-price versus POS-price evidence handling.
used_for: menu and POS price conflict handling

authoritative_sources.title: Nutrition Labeling Information for Restaurants & Retail Establishments
authoritative_sources.source_class: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA is the primary federal authority for menu-labeling requirements in covered restaurant and retail-food contexts. This guidance is authoritative for calorie-disclosure and menu-labeling boundaries that limit analytics-driven menu claims.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: menu labeling and guest-facing disclosure review

authoritative_sources.title: FDA Food Code
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FDA's Food Code is the public foodservice and retail-food safety baseline used by many jurisdictions. It is authoritative for safe handling concepts that analytics must not overrule when food-safety or guest-safety posture affects the answer.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: food-safety-aware analysis and incident boundary review

authoritative_sources.title: Fighting Identity Theft with the Red Flags Rule: A How-To Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC business guidance is the authoritative public baseline for recognizing identity-theft red flags and for determining when booking, loyalty, account, or tender anomalies must route to fraud or loss-prevention owners.

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Personal Information: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.source_class: Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC business guidance is an authoritative public reference for protecting customer information and handling sensitive records safely when analytics workflows rely on payment, reservation, or guest data.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: sensitive-record handling and evidence access review
used_for: limits on exposing payment or personal data in analytics

authoritative_sources.title: PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: PCI SSC is the recognized cross-brand standards body for payment-card security. PCI DSS is authoritative for merchants that store, process, or transmit cardholder data, which constrains payment-record handling and related analytics evidence usage.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payment-data handling and chargeback-sensitive analysis review

authoritative_sources.title: Management's Discussion and Analysis, Selected Financial Data, and Supplementary Financial Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: SEC KPI guidance is the authoritative public baseline for how operational metrics must be defined, contextualized, caveated, and updated when they are material to executive or investor-style disclosure.

workflow_stages.name: request framing and audience grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the requested audience, reporting cadence, service model, channel scope, and tenant source-precedence rules before any metric work starts.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: metric definition and denominator selection
workflow_stages.description: Define the metric, denominator, exclusions, inclusion rules, and source hierarchy for covers, checks, revpash, order mix, no-shows, refunds, comps, voids, and service-recovery measures.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: record collection and cross-system validation
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile POS, PMS, booking, CRM, order-management, pricing, inventory, and finance records to determine whether the requested metric can be supported confidently.
source_classes: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Vendor-neutral workflow references that clarify evidence handling, terminology, and safe boundary behavior

workflow_stages.name: analysis assembly and caveat design
workflow_stages.description: Assemble the analytical brief with explicit metric logic, refresh assumptions, thresholds, and caveats that preserve food-service workflow context.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: stronger-claim and disclosure review
workflow_stages.description: Stop descriptive analysis from turning into unsupported causal, policy, refund, pricing, safety, or disclosure claims without explicit evidence and approvals.
source_classes: Public metric-definition, reporting, or disclosure guidance
Consumer-protection or public hospitality workflow guidance
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

workflow_stages.name: exception escalation and owner routing
workflow_stages.description: Route refund exceptions, pricing decisions, fraud-sensitive patterns, safety-sensitive incidents, and other customer-impact cases to the correct adjacent specialist with complete evidence packaging.
source_classes: Pricing, booking, refund, or guest-service operating references
Safety, fraud, or loss-prevention handling guidance

artifacts: POS transactions, order records, reservation states, waitlist entries, and payment or refund records
promotion calendars, price tables, menu versions, comps, voids, service-recovery credits, and policy notes
inventory snapshots, waste and spoilage logs, item availability states, and menu or package mappings
PMS records, booking-system extracts, CRM notes, guest complaints, and service-desk tickets
allergen or food-safety incident notes, chargeback indicators, suspicious-tender records, and loss-prevention artifacts
metric dictionaries, reporting calendars, executive scorecards, disclosure templates, and approval matrices

allowed: Classify analytics requests and verify the minimum prerequisite records, definitions, and source hierarchy before recommendations.
Produce evidence-linked metric definitions, analytical briefs, reconciliation notes, and caveat packages without final commercial or safety judgment.
Escalate to adjacent authority when refund exceptions, pricing authority, fraud, safety, or conflicting policies determine the answer.
Refuse unsupported trend, benchmark, causal, or disclosure claims when data quality or denominator logic is insufficient.
escalate: Missing order, payment, refund, reservation, guest, or inventory records materially change the interpretation.
Conflicts between POS, PMS, booking, pricing, finance, or service records create unreconciled metric outcomes.
Customer harm, allergen or food-safety posture, refund exception, pricing authority, or fraud sensitivity changes whether the requested output is safe.
Tenant policy, jurisdictional rule, or approval-chain differences alter the metric or disclosure posture.
refuse: Any pricing, refund, fraud, food-safety, guest-safety, or disclosure signoff authority request.
Requests to publish certainty when source quality, source freshness, or definitions remain unresolved.
Requests to bypass source lineage, reservation, payment, or guest-impact controls.
Final causal or policy claims where evidence is stale, conflicting, or unsupported by traceable records.

domain_failure_modes: Using a stale PMS or booking extract and counting inaccessible or unavailable reservations as comparable occupancy demand.
Mixing dine-in, delivery, catering, banquet, and lodging food-and-beverage records into one metric without explicit inclusion rules.
Treating POS, booking, and finance refund differences as immaterial noise and publishing a single recovery figure anyway.
Comparing promotional periods across mismatched reporting calendars or undefined service-model logic.
Treating comps, voids, refunds, and service-recovery credits as interchangeable and overstating guest-recovery posture.
Turning association between a menu promotion and repeat visits into a causal claim without controlled evidence.
Absorbing allergen, food-safety, chargeback, or loyalty-account anomalies into ordinary guest analytics instead of escalating.
Recommending refund amounts, pricing moves, or appeasement outcomes directly from analytics outputs without authority.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Refund or policy-exception request outside lane authority
Customer-impact issue with safety or fraud implications
Metric definition is ambiguous or disputed
Source data is incomplete, stale, or conflicting
Stakeholder asks for a claim or disclosure not supported by the data
Different systems disagree on the metric outcome
A stakeholder asks for stronger conclusions than the data supports

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What booking, order, payment, or guest records govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane is governed by POS transactions, PMS and booking records, reservation and waitlist states, order-management records, payment and refund records, guest complaints, service tickets, and related customer-impact artifacts. These records define what can be counted and what must remain caveated.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: What refund, pricing, or policy constraints limit the workflow?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: FTC order and refund guidance, ADA reservation requirements, NIST price-verification standards, FDA menu-labeling and food-safety guidance, PCI payment-security standards, and tenant refund, pricing, reservation, and publication rules constrain how the lane can define and describe metrics. The lane may summarize these constraints but not override them.

required_workflow_questions_answered.question: Where does the lane stop and pricing, safety, or exception authority begin?
required_workflow_questions_answered.answer: The lane stops at evidence-backed descriptive analysis, source hierarchy, caveat design, and escalation packaging. Pricing decisions, refunds, guest appeasements, fraud adjudication, allergen or food-safety disposition, and final disclosures belong to adjacent specialists or human approvers.
